During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Pastor Miles McPherson shared a personal family story about his daughter's wedding and his 3-year-old granddaughter. She named him "Boppy," and she has a series of unique "Boppy-isms" that only the immediate family understands. One of them is her phrase "Boppy wo," which she uses whenever something exciting or sudden happens.

Pastor Miles explained this as a private, intimate family code. If he were in the hospital on his deathbed in intensive care, and the hospital staff were blocking visitors at the door, but his granddaughter came up and whispered "Boppy wo" at the door, his eyes would immediately light up and he would say, "Bring her in." It is a secret code of immediate recognition.

He explained that Jesus has His own "Boppy code" in scripture. When Jesus said certain things, He was speaking in a first-century cultural code that the people of His day immediately recognized as claiming to be God. Even if a modern reader misses the nuance, the high priest and religious leaders knew exactly what He was saying, which is why they tore their clothes and accused Him of blasphemy.

During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
In 2017, Pastor Miles' mother passed away from cancer. He and his sister took care of her, and he was there in the room when she took her last breath. After her passing, the mortician arrived to prepare her body, and Miles asked if he could go into the room with him.

Miles stood by as the mortician meticulously and respectfully wrapped his mother's body in a linen sheet, asking permission for each part: "Can I wrap her legs? Can I wrap her arms? Can I wrap her torso?"

But then, the mortician paused before her head. He asked: "Can I cover her head and put this over her face?" At that moment, Miles felt a deep chill and thought: "This is the final step. When he does this, it is over." He realized covering the face is the ultimate pronouncement of death.

When Jesus rose from the dead, he left the linen grave clothes behind, but he folded the head/face handkerchief in a separate place by itself. By removing the face cloth, Jesus was declaring: "I am not done. My face is not covered. I still have business to do, people to speak to, and glory to emanate."

Believers must make a definitive, aggressive decision to completely crucify and knock out the flesh rather than attempting to manage it through compromise.

When we accept His proposal, we are saved from our desperate situation.

During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Pastor Miles describes driving up the 15 freeway toward Pasadena, just past Escondido. From the top of a hill, hundreds of yards away, he spotted a dog running across the highway from the other side. The dog successfully crossed the median and ran onto Miles' side of the freeway. Right in front of the dog was a young girl driving a low Pontiac Fiero. Because she was only looking at the road directly in front of her car, she didn't see the dog coming until it ran right into her path. She slammed on her brakes, tires smoking, and ran over and killed the dog. She pulled over, red-faced and crying, completely devastated by the accident. Miles didn't hit the dog because, from his vantage point on top of the hill, he saw the big picture and watched the dog's path from the very beginning.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: In our relationships and life decisions, we often get in trouble because we focus only on what is immediately in front of us—our current feelings, desires, and immediate circumstances. We make frantic, short-sighted choices that lead to emotional crash-ups. God, however, sits at the top of the hill and sees the entire big picture of our lives. We must trust His bird's-eye perspective and follow His rules instead of relying on our narrow, localized vision.

Mutual submission and love are the keys to a flourishing, Christ-centered marriage.

We are called to be the chief servants in our homes, reflecting how Christ served and laid His life down for the church.

When God speaks, His words carry creative, redemptive, and transformative power. When His words are spoken into our lives, they act as the ultimate "key" that ignites our true individual identity, shapes our morality, and sets our calling in motion.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Divine Appointment, NFL, Bengals, Chris Henry, Legacy
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Pastor Miles shares a story about NFL player Chris Henry of the Cincinnati Bengals, who passed away in late 2009. While the public and critics knew Chris Henry through his troubled record—being arrested five times in one year and suspended—the team had a completely different perspective. Running back Cedric Benson pointed out that Chris sat next to him for years and never said a single negative thing about another player. Another teammate shared how Chris lit up showing him New Orleans and discussing Hurricane Katrina. A coach shared how Chris sat in the pouring rain in the bleachers to watch his son's high school football game. On the day he died, the coach's daughter was actually in a Christian bookstore getting Chris's name engraved on a Bible.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: We frequently judge people by their failures and worst chapters. But God's grace continually reaches out to people, even to the last second of their lives, because of His ultimate Promise. Chris was walking in a new direction and turning his life around when his life was cut short. God keeps His promise to seek out the lost, love us despite our "junk," and give everyone an opportunity to be restored.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Barren Women, Old Testament, Mary, Virgin Birth, Miracles, Sarah
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: The Old Testament builds a perfect foundation for the New Testament, especially concerning the virgin birth. Throughout the Old Testament, God repeatedly selected barren women who could not biologically have children and made a promise to give them a child. Abraham's wife Sarah was a 90-year-old woman walking with a cane when God promised her a baby. She laughed in disbelief, but she eventually gave birth to Isaac. Isaac's wife Rebecca was barren, as was Jacob's wife Rachel. Samson's mother and Samuel's mother (Hannah) were also barren. Each had a supernatural birth ordained by God, establishing that the children belonged to God's divine purpose. Elizabeth, John the Baptist's mother, was also barren and had a child in her old age.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: All of these miraculous Old Testament births prepared the world for the ultimate supernatural birth: Mary's virgin birth. Mary was a young virgin who had never been with a man, and her pregnancy was biologically impossible. Yet, the Holy Spirit overshadowed her, establishing that with God, absolutely nothing is impossible. Just as God has been faithful to perform the impossible through barren women throughout history, we can trust Him to do the impossible in our lives.

One of the high school girls stopped attending for several weeks. When Miles called her to check in and find out what was wrong, she sadly told him, "God can't even help me with my problem." When Miles asked, "Are you pregnant?" she answered, "Kinda." Miles calls this "biological comedy" because it is physically impossible to be kinda pregnant—you either are or you aren't. The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Lordship is an all-or-nothing reality, just like pregnancy. You cannot selectively choose which commandments to obey and which to ignore while calling Jesus your "Lord." Half-hearted, selective obedience is actually complete disobedience. True lordship requires complete, absolute submission of every area of our lives to His authority.

During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: While walking on the beach on a Saturday, Pastor Miles spotted a small bird standing on one leg in the wet sand. Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Gibbons Monkey, Trap, Coconut, Candy, Surrender, Money, Pride
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: In Africa, hunters catch the small, highly active Gibbons monkey using a hollowed-out metal coconut chained to a tree. They put rice and candy inside the coconut. The monkey smells the sweet food, reaches his hand inside the small hole, and grabs a huge handful. However, because his fist is now clenched, he cannot pull his hand back out of the narrow hole. Despite screaming, grunting, and pulling all day as the hunter calmly approaches with a gun, the monkey refuses to let go of the food. All the monkey had to do to be completely free was open his hand and let go of the candy.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: This is the ultimate picture of how we clench our hands around our "possessions" (money, bad habits, toxic relationships, pride). We are trapped and spiritually defeated, screaming in pain as the enemy closes in on us, entirely because we refuse to let go of the "candy" in our hands. Lordship means opening our hands, surrendering everything we cling to, and letting God have absolute control.

While this line is often celebrated as the height of romantic sentiment, Miles challenges the audience to look at it through a biblical lens.

He points out that in the movie, Jerry Maguire is a broken, desperate man who is looking for a woman to fill the empty holes in his soul. The world teaches that we are all "half-people" wandering around looking for our "other half" to make us whole. Miles argues that this Hollywood standard creates a fantasy that places an impossible burden on a spouse to provide a sense of identity and fulfillment that they were never meant to provide.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Prison, Future, Forgiveness
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: While visiting Calipatria State Prison, a knife fight broke out. Loud music blocked the sound of sirens, and the guards forced all inmates onto the ground (or be targets for snipers). Tear gas and rubber bullets were deployed. Later, in the Protective Custody yard with sex offenders, God told Miles to stay silent and look at them for two minutes. God showed him that He is not sadness-focused on their past, but excited about their potential from this day forward.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: God doesn't look at us with grief over our past sins or failures, but with excitement for what we can become from this day forward when we surrender our lives to Him.

1. The Calipatria State Prison Riot and PC Yard

Story / Illustration:Pastor Miles recounts a visit to Calipatria State Prison, a maximum-security facility. During a time of high tension, a riot broke out, highlighting the stark divisions within the prison walls. He specifically mentions the "PC Yard" or Protective Custody yard. This area is reserved for inmates who are targets of violence from the general population, often including sex offenders. In the hierarchy of prison culture, these individuals are the most despised, isolated even from the community of criminals.

Spiritual Metaphor:The donkey in the scripture was "tied up" at a place where two roads met, representing a life of restriction and labels. Just as the men in the PC yard are defined and bound by their worst mistakes and the labels society gives them, we are often tied up by our sins. The metaphor emphasizes that Jesus deliberately sends for the one who is "tied up"—the one society has cast aside—to be loosed for His divine service.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Service, Palm Sunday, Christ
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: On Palm Sunday, as Jesus entered Jerusalem, He rode on a young donkey. The donkey walked on fine robes and palm branches while crowds cheered and shouted "Hosanna!" The donkey got to experience all this incredible glory, but it was only because he was carrying Jesus on his back.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: We are like the donkey. We can never be God, but if we carry Jesus and let Him lead us, we get to experience the miraculous and amazing things He does. People might even give us credit, but we must always remember that we are just the donkey carrying the King.

His Wife's COVID & The White Dove: Admitting You are Wrong in Marriage
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Wife, Marriage, Pride, Communication
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Pastor Miles asks the men in the congregation if they have ever had an argument with their wife or girlfriend, realized they were completely wrong, and found it nearly impossible to admit it. They usually blame the wife instead of taking responsibility.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Christ sets us free from our pride and the bondage of our egos. True freedom in Christ means having the humility to say, "I was wrong," to repent, and to offer or receive forgiveness.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Daughter, Newborn, Parents
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: When his first daughter was born, her head was soft and had a distinct point on the side (like Gumby) from passing through the birth canal. Twelve hours after she was born, she was wrapped in a blanket in her incubator with only her pointy head sticking out. Miles stood there, completely captivated and busy watching her, because she belonged to him.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: We belong to God, and He watches over us with the same intense, loving pride of a father looking at his newborn child. He is excited about our specific purpose, even with our soft-headed "points" and imperfections.
During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Story / Illustration:Miles recalls the birth of his daughter and the initial shock of seeing her head shape. Because of the pressure of the birth canal, her head appeared elongated and "pointy," resembling the character Gumby. As a new father, he was concerned, but the doctor assured him that over time, the head would round out and take its proper shape.

Spiritual Metaphor:When we are first "untied" or born again, we might look a bit "pointy" or misshapen spiritually. We carry the marks of the "birth" process and our old life. This illustration teaches that God is a patient physician who continues to shape us. We shouldn't be discouraged by our initial awkwardness in faith, as God is constantly molding us into the image of His Son.

His Wife's COVID & The White Dove: Hepatitis C Ink and Wet Rag
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Prison, Sickness, Pride
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: While ministering in the prison, Miles learned about the high rates of Hepatitis C among inmates due to sharing needles or contaminated ink. He also heard how inmates dip knives in feces to poison their targets. After hearing these details, Miles went into the yard to shake hands with inmates. One inmate came up holding a wet rag and asked Miles to sign it. When Miles signed it, his hand got wet, and he panicked, wondering what biological hazard was on that wet rag.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: The Bible says our self-righteousness is like filthy rags (Isaiah 64:6) - dirty, contaminated, and potentially poisonous. We cannot present our own good works to God as righteousness; we must be clothed in the perfect righteousness that Christ provides.

5. The Hepatitis C Ink and Wet Rag in Prison

Story / Illustration:In the prison system, inmates often mark themselves with "ink" or tattoos that signify their gang affiliations or their past identities. Miles describes how some prisoners, in an attempt to change their lives or hide their past, would use a wet rag and harsh methods to try and scrub away or "wipe" a signature or mark. This process was often dangerous, involving shared needles and contaminated ink that led to the spread of Hepatitis C.

Spiritual Metaphor:Human efforts to erase our past "signatures" are often messy, dangerous, and ineffective. We cannot scrub away the stains of our history on our own. The metaphor points to Jesus as the only one who can truly cleanse us. He doesn't just wipe at the surface; His blood provides a deep cleansing that removes the spiritual "ink" of our past without the destructive consequences of human effort.

The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: In a world of shifting sand and muddy pits, God takes our feet out of the "miry clay" and places them on a Rock-solid foundation (Psalm 40:2). Our steps are established when we are anchored to Christ, the Rock of our salvation.

6. Jackie Gleason and Little Boys Throwing Rocks

Story / Illustration:Miles shares a story involving the famous comedian Jackie Gleason. In the narrative, a group of little boys is throwing rocks at a house, and one of the boys eventually gets caught. Instead of the expected wrath or punishment from the homeowner, the boy is met with an unexpected act of kindness and mercy.

He lay in a tiny preschool bed with his feet hanging off one end, his head hanging off the other, wrapped in a Spider-Man blanket. The kids were laughing loudly at the sight.

Where the Spirit Rests: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Sickness, Balance, Hearing
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Pastor Miles explains that he is experiencing ear, nose, and throat (ENT) issues, causing him to feel dizzy in the mornings. He consulted a doctor who explained that the inner ear contains fluid that maintains balance. There are tiny hairs in the ear called stereocilia that touch this fluid and send messages to the brain about the head's angle. If an infection occurs, the fluid gets thick or moves slowly, causing the ear to send delayed or incorrect messages to the brain. This creates a conflict: the eyes see that the head is upright, but the inner ear says it's tilted, causing the brain to spin in confusion.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Every day, our spirit tries to line up God's will with our own. When our spiritual receptors (our "stereocilia") are clogged by sin, distraction, or a bad attitude, we receive a distorted signal. We must align ourselves with God's truth to regain our spiritual balance, especially when coming to church with a bad attitude.
During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Story / Illustration:Pastor Miles describes a visit to an Ear, Nose, and Throat (ENT) specialist to discuss the mechanics of human balance. He explains that the inner ear contains a specific fluid and microscopic "hairs" known as stereocilia. When a person moves, the fluid shifts, bending these stereocilia, which then send signals to the brain to indicate orientation. Dizziness or vertigo occurs when the fluid continues to spin or the hairs remain bent even when the body is stationary. This physical disorientation creates a sensation that the world is spinning, regardless of the objective reality of the ground beneath one's feet.

Spiritual Metaphor:This serves as a metaphor for spiritual disorientation. When an individual's "internal sensors" are damaged by sin or focused on the world's shifting values rather than God’s truth, they experience a spiritual vertigo. Just as a person with an inner ear infection cannot trust their own senses to find the horizon, humanity cannot rely on its own feelings to find moral and spiritual truth. We require an external, fixed point—God’s Word—to regain our balance.

Where the Spirit Rests: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Ark, Stats, Flood
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Miles shares details about the scale of Noah's Ark. It was 450 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high (three stories)—strikingly similar to the building Rock Church was preparing to purchase (444 feet long, 180 feet wide, and 45 feet high). He explains that the Ark wasn't meant for navigation, only to float. Noah didn't need full-grown elephants or every single breed of dog. He only brought the ancestral "kinds" (genus level, e.g., two ancestral dogs to reproduce all modern dogs). Statistically, the Ark had the volume of 522 train boxcars, while 35,000 sheep-sized animals would easily fit into just 146 boxcars.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: God's instructions and provisions are incredibly practical and mathematically precise, even when they seem impossible or "crazy" to the world. When God calls us to a task, His provision is more than sufficient to preserve us and carry us through the storm.

Where the Spirit Rests: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Science, Fossils, Flood
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: To defend the historical reality of the global flood, Miles cites the fossil record. He notes that fossils can only form if an animal is buried rapidly in an airtight environment (like mud) before decaying. He highlights the Miocene shale beds in California, where more than one billion herring fish (averaging 6 to 8 inches in length) died and were fossilized within a four-square-mile area.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: The physical evidence of God's cataclysmic judgment is visible all around us in the earth. The flood is a real historical warning of judgment, but it also highlights the reality of God's salvation and covenant for those who believe.
During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Story / Illustration:To conclude the sermon, Pastor Miles describes the physical reality of the Roman crucifixion, specifically focusing on the nails used. He references the historical use of heavy, tapered iron nails, approximately nine inches in length. These were driven through the wrists and feet of the victim to secure them to the wooden cross. The sheer size and weight of such a nail paint a vivid, visceral picture of the agony involved in the execution.

Spiritual Metaphor:The nine-inch nail represents the "cost of the Ark." While Noah’s Ark was made of gopher wood and pitch, the "Ark" of our salvation was made of the wood of the cross and the blood of Christ. The nail bridges the gap between the judgment of Noah's day and the grace of the New Covenant. God’s judgment is real, but His love drove Him to endure the nine-inch nails so that we could find safety in Him.

His Wife's COVID & The White Dove: PBS Journey of Sperm and Egg
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Biology, Birth, Children
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Miles describes watching a PBS documentary called Life's Greatest Miracle about how babies are made. He explains the journey of the sperm swimming towards the egg. Inside the woman's tubes, tiny hairs called cilia wave back and forth, filtering the sperm. They check the sperm's DNA, and let only the healthy ones pass while holding back the rest. When the surviving sperm reach the egg, they hit a protective shell called the Zona. To get in, the sperm's head must perfectly match the shape of protein receptors sticking out of the Zona. When a match is made, a chemical reaction triggers, dissolving the head of the sperm to release a protein that burns a hole in the Zona, allowing entry and instantly closing the shell to others.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: This microscopic, highly complex process is scientifically undeniable yet mathematically impossible to attribute to random chance. It is a supernatural design. We must realize that our very existence is a miracle of God. When we face difficulties, we must thank God that we even survived the womb and are alive today.
During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Story / Illustration:Drawing from the PBS documentary Life's Greatest Miracle, Pastor Miles recounts the harrowing and statistically improbable journey of a single sperm cell attempting to fertilize an egg. Out of millions, only one makes it past the various obstacles of the reproductive tract. The documentary captures the complexity and the "miracle" of life's beginning, showing how every detail must be perfectly synchronized for a new human being to be formed.

Spiritual Metaphor:This illustration highlights the intentionality of creation. If the process of physical birth is so complex and carefully orchestrated by God, how much more intentional is the process of spiritual rebirth? It underscores that no one is an accident; God’s hand is in the details of our existence and His pursuit of our hearts. Salvation is the "greatest miracle" of all, requiring a divine intervention to bring life where there was none.

Where the Spirit Rests: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Sickness, Cross, Perspective
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: An inmate or contact recently gave Miles a metal nail the size of the ones used to crucify Jesus—nine inches long. Miles held it and immediately realized, "I have no problems." He suggests that if anyone brought their complaints to a counselor, and the counselor asked them to hold a nine-inch nail against their wrist and said they'd be nailed to a tree in an hour, their perspective would change instantly.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Our daily complaints and problems are trivial and highly fixable compared to the agony Christ suffered to save us. Keeping our eyes on the cross eliminates self-pity and fills us with gratitude.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Skills, Diving, Shark
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: While visiting his sister in Tortola for nine days, Pastor Miles and his family went snorkeling daily. Eager to go deeper, they took a beginner's scuba diving course. Before going down to 40 feet, they had to learn essential skills, such as how to clear water out of a mask underwater (fingers on top, blow out of nose, tilt head back) and how to recover a lost mouthpiece without swallowing water. While diving, they encountered a shark resting on the bottom. The instructor gave the hand sign for a shark (hand on head like a fin). He swam down and touched the shark, which jumped and swam away. Miles tried to touch it too, but the shark swam off before he could reach it.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Extraordinary dreams require extraordinary skills. Just as diving in deep water requires mastery of specific survival skills beforehand, Joseph's dream only came true because he spent years in Egypt perfecting his skills in management and dream interpretation. We must identify and sharpen our God-given strengths.
During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Story / Illustration:Pastor Miles shared a story about visiting his sister in Tortola, British Virgin Islands. During the trip, he participated in a scuba diving course. Part of the essential training involved learning specific hand signals for underwater communication. One particular sign taught was the signal for a shark: placing the hand vertically on top of the head like a dorsal fin. While actually diving in the ocean, the training became reality when Miles encountered a shark. He had to use that specific hand sign to alert the rest of his group.

Spiritual Metaphor:The dream requires preparation and environmental awareness. Just as a diver must learn the language of the ocean before entering it, a dreamer must learn the "signs" of the environment God has placed them in. You must be able to identify the realities and dangers of your surroundings to navigate them successfully.

2. The Pop Warner Running Back Collision and Blocking for Lorenzo

Story / Illustration:In his youth, Miles played Pop Warner football as a star running back. He was used to the glory of carrying the ball and scoring. However, his coach eventually gave him a different assignment: he was told he had to block for another player named Lorenzo. Miles initially resisted the idea, preferring the spotlight of being the primary runner. Despite his personal desires, he had to lower his shoulder and take the hits so that Lorenzo could find the opening to run.

Spiritual Metaphor:A skill of a dreamer is the willingness to serve someone else's dream. Before Joseph was promoted to the palace, he spent years serving the dreams of others—the jailer, the cupbearer, and the baker. Character is built when you are willing to "block" for others and support the team's success rather than just seeking your own.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Football, Sports, Hard Times
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Miles shares his love for football, recalling playing running back in Pop Warner and high school. He notes that a running back's goal is the goal line, and they expect to get hit and knocked down along the way. Miles played alongside his friend Lorenzo. While he loved carrying the ball, he loved blocking for Lorenzo even more. If an opponent was standing between Lorenzo and the goal line, Miles would actively seek out the collision to knock them down. He notes that if you don't want to get hit, you should play basketball instead.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: In life and ministry, we cannot avoid obstacles, confrontations, or hard times. Instead of fearing them, we must welcome collisions as God's training ground to sharpen us, build our endurance, and prepare us for the next level.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Pride, Discipline, Correction
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Miles asks the women in the audience if they have an emery board. They use its rough, abrasive surface to grind down corns, calluses, or rough heels on their feet.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Our hard-headed pride and arrogance cannot be smoothed out by gentle means. God uses circumstances and other people—rough, abrasive tools—to grind down our rough edges. Just as "iron sharpens iron," we need other people to speak the truth in love and keep us sharp.
During the height of the COVID-19 pandem:
Story / Illustration:Pastor Miles used the physical example of an emery board and its effect on the skin. He described how over time, the heels of the feet can develop thick, hard, and rough calluses. To remove this dead skin and make the heel smooth again, one must use an abrasive emery board to sand it down. The process involves friction and the scraping away of the old, rough layers.

Spiritual Metaphor:God uses "abrasive" people and difficult circumstances as spiritual emery boards. These experiences are designed to sand off the rough edges of our character. The calluses of pride, impatience, and selfishness must be removed through the friction of life's trials so that we are refined and prepared for the dream God has for us.

During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Miles describes watching a video of Martin Luther King Jr. preaching in his church. King was so captured by the fire of his sermon that he actually walked away from the pulpit and continued preaching from the floor, entirely consumed by the passion of the message.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: Fulfilling a God-given dream requires absolute passion and fire. We must be so aligned with God's purpose that we are consumed by it, working diligently even when no one is looking.

4. Martin Luther King Jr. Leaving the Pulpit

Story / Illustration:Miles reflected on the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. While Dr. King was a powerful and gifted preacher within the church, his dream for civil rights could not be fulfilled solely from behind the pulpit. He had to physically leave the safety and comfort of the church walls and go into the streets, organize boycotts, and march in the face of opposition.

Spiritual Metaphor:A dreamer must bridge the gap between the spiritual vision and practical execution. It is not enough to simply "have" a dream or talk about it in a spiritual setting. Fulfilling a God-given vision requires the courage to step out of comfortable, religious environments and into the "streets" where the work must actually be done.

Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Speech, Discipline, Humility
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Miles recalls meeting with two coaches from Toastmasters about a year and a half ago. They explained that in Toastmasters meetings, speakers are fined a quarter every time they say "um" or "uh." Miles jokes that since that meeting, he hears a voice counting his "ums" every time he preaches, and he probably owes them $10,000. He contrasts this with an apologist debater he heard, who spoke flawlessly for 20 minutes in a high-pressure debate without saying "um" a single time.
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: The process of training, refining your communication, and receiving critique is incredibly humbling. However, if we refuse to undergo the humbling process of feedback, we will never achieve the excellence required to stand before kings and fulfill our calling.

His Wife's COVID & The White Dove: The Billy Graham Secret Service Bullet Shield
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Sacrifice, Protection, Jesus
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Prior to speaking at his Qualcomm Stadium Crusade, Pastor Miles got to spend a few minutes backstage with Billy Graham. Graham was very weak and traveling in a golf cart. As he got to the lift taking him to the stage, a Secret Service guard stood directly in front of him. Miles realized that this guard was positioned specifically to take a bullet for Graham in the event of an assassination attempt. Miles walked up to the guard, saying, "God bless you for what you do. If anyone wanted to shoot him, they'd have to shoot you first."
The Holy Spirit brings comfort and prese: This is a physical picture of Jesus Christ's protection and ultimate sacrifice. Christ took the "bullet" of sin and death for us on the cross, taking our place so that we could be saved and shielded from judgment.

His Wife's COVID & The White Dove: The Closet Escape and the Realities of Motherhood
Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospita: Wife, Marriage, Family, Children, Sacrifice
During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 1: Pastor Miles reads a suggestion from a marriage retreat packet meant to spark romance: unannounced, pull your wife into a closet, hold her tight, and whisper, "Pack a light bag with some nice outfits. In the next 24 hours, you're going to be swept away to an undisclosed romantic location." Miles jokes that whoever wrote this has no concept of a mother's mind. For a mother to enjoy that, you must append: "And by the way, I have already arranged the food, packed the kids' vitamins, hired the babysitter, and left our contact number!"

The Story:In his message, Pastor Miles describes the historical character of the Pharisees, whom he calls the "Phabulous Pharisees." The word Pharisee literally means "separated one." They were highly educated, strictly trained rabbis who wore special, elaborate robes to signal their holiness to everyone who saw them. As part of their outward display of piety, they wore a leather strap wrapped around their heads with a small, domed leather box (called a phylactery) positioned directly on their foreheads. Inside this box was a small scroll containing the written word of God. They wore an identical leather strap and box wrapped around their wrists.

These boxes were meant to represent that the word of God was constantly on their minds and guiding their physical actions. Miles jokes that this would be the modern equivalent of a pastor walking around with a giant physical Bible strapped directly to his head with duct tape, telling everyone, "Look how holy I am!"

The Spiritual Metaphor:This story illustrates the danger of hollow, outward religious performance. The Pharisees focused on physical, mechanical representations of holiness while their hearts were far from God. God does not want showy, superficial displays of piety; He desires a "circumcised heart"—an inward reality where our love for Him is authentic, and our thoughts and actions naturally align with His word without the need for theatrical display.

The Story:During his college years, the university's varsity baseball team was known for being in spectacular athletic conditioning. Their standard training regimen included running a grueling, hilly two-to-three-mile course through the steep neighborhood streets of New Haven, Connecticut. Priding himself on his own elite physical fitness, Miles asked the baseball players if he could run the course with them. They agreed, and Miles set out with the team.

Miles suffered from exercise-induced asthma, which he knew would make the hilly run extremely painful. As soon as they stepped outside the gym doors, the baseball team's leader took off at a full sprint. They soon reached a hill so steep it felt completely vertical. As they ran, Miles' lungs began to burn intensely, his asthma flared up, and his body screamed in agony. However, his athletic pride kicked in, and he repeated to himself: "There is no way on earth I am losing to a baseball player. That is completely unacceptable." Refusing to quit or even slow down, Miles pressed through the excruciating pain, matching the leader step-for-step, and finished the run tied for first place.

The Spiritual Metaphor:Running the Christian life is not a casual jog; it is a grueling, painful, and disciplined race that requires us to push past physical and emotional agony. To finish well, we must run in a way to win. We must discipline our bodies, bring our fleshly desires into absolute subjection, and refuse to allow pain, exhaustion, or asthma-like spiritual blockages to stop us from crossing the finish line of our high calling.

During the height of the COVID-19 pandem: Miles describes the incredible, dramatic timing of the bad news delivered to Job. While he was still speaking, a second ran to say fire from heaven consumed the sheep. While he was still speaking, a third ran to say the Chaldeans raided the camels. While he was still speaking, a fourth ran to say a wind collapsed the house on his children. Job had four guys running to him in a timed relay of absolute devastation, waiting to see if he would crack.
